1:
"In the third month, when the children of Israel were gone forth out of the land of Egypt, the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai." The witnesses note Israel's arrival at Sinai in the third month. On "In the third month, when the children of Israel were gone forth out of the land of Egypt," the grammatical voices note the time. Henry and Gill note that "in the third month... the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai" — Israel arrived at the wilderness of Sinai in the third month after the Exodus (about the time of Pentecost, fifty days from the Passover); the journey to the mount of God (Exodus 3:12, where Moses was promised they would serve God); the providence (the people brought to the place of the covenant).

On "the same day," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the time. Calvin and JFB note the time (the third month, the new moon, or the same day) — the arrival at Sinai about fifty days from the Exodus; the witnesses note the providential timing (the law given at Sinai about the season later kept as Pentecost, the feast of weeks; the Spirit given at Pentecost, Acts 2, the law written on the heart, a contrast and fulfillment).

On "came they into the wilderness of Sinai," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the place: "came they into the wilderness of Sinai" — the wilderness of Sinai (the mount of God, Horeb, Exodus 3:1); the place of the covenant and the law (vv.3-25; ch. 20); the providence (Israel brought to Sinai to receive the law, the covenant established). The verse displays Israel's arrival at Sinai in the third month.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the arrival — "In the third month... the same day came they into the wilderness of Sinai," the time (Exodus 3:12, Henry, Gill); "the same day," the timing (Acts 2, Calvin, JFB); "came they into the wilderness of Sinai," the place (Exodus 3:1, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arriving Israel. The verse marks the time: third month. The verse displays the wilderness: of Sinai. The verse displays the mount: of God. The verse displays the covenant: at hand. The arrival displays Israel brought to the place of the covenant. The verse displays the Israel: arriving. The verse displays the Sinai: reached. The verse displays the law: to come. Israel's arrival at Sinai in the third month displays the bringing to the covenant: in the third month they came to Sinai (Exodus 3:1, 12; Acts 2) — the people brought to the mount of God, the place of the law. The verse displays the camp before the mount (v.2).
2:
"For they were departed from Rephidim, and were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness; and there Israel camped before the mount." The very brief verse notes Israel encamping before Mount Sinai. The grammatical voices note the camp. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they were departed from Rephidim, and were come to the desert of Sinai... and there Israel camped before the mount" — Israel, having left Rephidim (the place of the water and the battle with Amalek, ch. 17), came to the desert of Sinai and camped before the mount (Sinai/Horeb); the encampment at the holy mount; the place of the covenant; the people gathered before the mount of God.

On "were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness," the witnesses note the place. Gill notes the desert of Sinai (the wilderness before the mount) — the encampment; the gathering of the people.

On "there Israel camped before the mount," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the mount: "there Israel camped before the mount" — Israel before Mount Sinai (the mount of God, Exodus 3:1; 19:1); the camp at the foot of the holy mount; the providence (the people gathered before the mount where God would descend, vv.16-20; the law given, ch. 20). The verse displays Israel encamping before Mount Sinai.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the camp — "were come to the desert of Sinai... and there Israel camped before the mount," the encampment (Exodus 3:1, Keil, Gill); "were come to the desert of Sinai, and had pitched in the wilderness," the place (Gill); "there Israel camped before the mount," the mount (vv.16-20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the encamping Israel. The verse marks the camp: before the mount. The verse displays the desert: of Sinai. The verse displays the mount: of God. The verse displays the covenant: at hand. The camp displays Israel before the holy mount. The verse displays the Israel: encamping. The verse displays the Sinai: before them. The verse displays the law: to come. Israel encamping before Mount Sinai displays the gathering at the holy mount: Israel camped before the mount (Exodus 3:1; vv.16-20) — the people at the foot of Sinai, where God would descend and give the law. The verse displays Moses going up to God (v.3).
3:
"And Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain, saying, Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel;" The witnesses note Moses going up to God and the LORD's call from the mount. On "Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain," the grammatical voices note the ascent. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain" — Moses went up the mount (toward God), and the LORD called to him from the mountain; the mediator going up to God (Moses the go-between, ascending to receive God's word); the call (God speaking from the mount); the office of Moses (the mediator of the covenant, Galatians 3:19).

On "Moses went up unto God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the mediator. Calvin and Wesley note Moses going up to God (the mediator, ascending the mount) — the office (Moses the channel between God and the people); the going-up to receive the word.

On "Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the message: "Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel" — the message for Israel (the house of Jacob, the children of Israel); the covenant proposal (vv.4-6); the providence (the word that Moses would carry down to the people, the covenant offered). The verse displays Moses going up to God and the LORD's call from the mount.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ascent — "Moses went up unto God, and the LORD called unto him out of the mountain... Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob," the mediator (Galatians 3:19, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses went up unto God," the mediator (Calvin, Wesley); "Thus shalt thou say to the house of Jacob, and tell the children of Israel," the message (vv.4-6, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ascending Moses. The verse marks the ascent: up to God. The verse displays the call: from the mount. The verse displays the message: for Israel. The verse displays the mediator: Moses. The ascent displays Moses the mediator going up to God. The verse displays the Moses: ascending. The verse displays the LORD: calling. The verse displays the covenant: proposed. Moses going up to God and the LORD's call from the mount display the office of the mediator: Moses went up, the LORD called, Thus shalt thou say to Israel (Galatians 3:19; vv.4-6) — the mediator ascending to receive the covenant word for the people. The verse displays the eagles' wings (v.4).
4:
"Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians, and how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself." The witnesses note God bearing Israel on eagles' wings — the crux of the tender deliverance. On "Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians," the grammatical voices note the witness. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Ye have seen what I did unto the Egyptians" — God appealed to what Israel had seen (the plagues, the Red Sea, the deliverance); the witnessed works (the ground of the covenant, God's prior grace); the appeal to experience (the redemption already wrought).

On "how I bare you on eagles' wings," interpreters expound the figure. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great image — "how I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself": God bore Israel as on eagles' wings (the tender, mighty, protecting care of God in the deliverance); the witnesses note the figure (the eagle stirring up her nest, bearing her young on her wings, Deuteronomy 32:11-12, "as an eagle... beareth them on her wings: so the LORD alone did lead him") — the eagle's strong, swift, lofty flight (the speed and power of the deliverance); the eagle bearing her young on her wings (not in her talons, but on her back, the tender, protective care, sheltering them from below); the lesson — God's mighty and tender care of his people (carrying them out of Egypt, over every danger, swiftly and safely); the parental love of God (bearing his people as an eagle her young, Isaiah 63:9, "in his love and in his pity he redeemed them... he bare them, and carried them").

On "and brought you unto myself," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the end. Wesley and Ellicott note "brought you unto myself" — the end of the deliverance (God brought Israel to himself, to the mount, into covenant, into his service and fellowship); the redemption's purpose (not merely out of Egypt, but unto God, into communion with him); the providence (the people brought to God, to be his own, vv.5-6; a type of the redemption that brings us to God, 1 Peter 3:18, "that he might bring us to God"). The verse displays God bearing Israel on eagles' wings.

The witnesses agree the substance: God reminded Israel he had borne them on eagles' wings and brought them to himself (Keil, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the tender deliverance: "I bare you on eagles' wings" — the mighty, tender, protecting care of God in the deliverance (Deuteronomy 32:11-12; Isaiah 63:9), the eagle bearing her young, the swift and safe redemption; "brought you unto myself" — the end of the redemption, to bring Israel to God, into covenant and fellowship (1 Peter 3:18). The verse displays the eagles'-wings crux. The verse marks the bearing: on eagles' wings. The verse displays the care: tender and mighty. The verse displays the end: unto myself. The verse displays the redemption: to God. The bearing displays God's tender care carrying his people. The verse displays the LORD: bearing. The verse displays the Israel: borne. The verse displays the end: unto God. God bearing Israel on eagles' wings displays the crux: I bare you on eagles' wings, and brought you unto myself (Deuteronomy 32:11-12; Isaiah 63:9; 1 Peter 3:18) — the mighty and tender care of the deliverance, the redemption that brings his people to God. The verse displays the covenant condition (v.5).
5:
"Now therefore, if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people: for all the earth is mine:" The witnesses note the covenant condition and Israel as God's peculiar treasure. On "if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant," the grammatical voices note the condition. Ellicott and Gill note that "if ye will obey my voice indeed, and keep my covenant, then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me" — God set the covenant condition: if Israel would obey his voice and keep his covenant, they would be his peculiar treasure; the covenant terms (obedience the condition of the blessing); the engagement (the covenant of Sinai, the people called to obedience).

On "ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people," the reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the treasure. Calvin and Wesley note "a peculiar treasure unto me above all people" — Israel God's "peculiar treasure" (Hebrew segullah, a special possession, a treasured property, a jewel) above all people; the election (Israel chosen, set apart, God's own special people, Deuteronomy 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; Psalm 135:4); the lesson — God's people his treasured possession (the church his jewels, Malachi 3:17, "they shall be mine... in that day when I make up my jewels"; the peculiar people, Titus 2:14; 1 Peter 2:9, "a peculiar people"); the value God sets on his own.

On "for all the earth is mine," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the sovereignty: "for all the earth is mine" — the ground of the choice (God, who owns all the earth, chose Israel for his own, not from need, but of grace; the sovereign election, choosing one nation though all are his); the sovereignty (all the earth God's, yet Israel his special treasure); the providence (the free, sovereign choice of grace). The verse displays the covenant condition and Israel as God's peculiar treasure.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the condition — "if ye will obey my voice... then ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me... for all the earth is mine," the covenant and treasure (Deuteronomy 7:6, Ellicott, Gill); "ye shall be a peculiar treasure unto me above all people," the treasure (Malachi 3:17; 1 Peter 2:9, Calvin, Wesley); "for all the earth is mine," the sovereignty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the treasured Israel. The verse marks the condition: obey and keep. The verse displays the treasure: peculiar. The verse displays the election: of grace. The verse displays the sovereignty: all the earth. The condition displays Israel God's treasured possession. The verse displays the Israel: treasured. The verse displays the covenant: conditioned. The verse displays the LORD: sovereign. The covenant condition and Israel as God's peculiar treasure display the election: if ye obey, ye shall be a peculiar treasure above all people (Deuteronomy 7:6; Malachi 3:17; 1 Peter 2:9) — God's special possession, his jewels, chosen of sovereign grace. The verse displays the kingdom of priests (v.6).
6:
"And ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. These are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel." The witnesses note Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation — the crux of the priestly people. On "ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests," interpreters expound the great word. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the title — "ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation": God designed Israel to be a kingdom of priests and a holy nation; the witnesses expound — a "kingdom of priests" (a royal priesthood, all the people in a sense priests, near to God, offering spiritual service, mediating between God and the nations; the whole nation consecrated to God's service, though the Aaronic priesthood was a special order within); the lesson — the priestly calling of God's people (the church a royal priesthood, the fulfillment in the New Testament, 1 Peter 2:9, "ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people"; Revelation 1:6, "hath made us kings and priests unto God"; 5:10; 20:6); the access of all believers to God (the priesthood of all the faithful, offering the sacrifices of praise, Hebrews 13:15; Romans 12:1); the nation set apart for God's worship and service.

On "and an holy nation," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the holiness. Wesley and Keil note "an holy nation" — Israel a nation set apart (consecrated, holy, separated unto God from the nations); the holiness (the people called to be holy, as God is holy, Leviticus 11:44-45; 19:2; 1 Peter 1:15-16); the separation unto God (the holy nation, distinct from the world, dedicated to God's service).

On "these are the words which thou shalt speak," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the message: "these are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel" — the covenant words for Israel (the high calling, the priestly and holy nation); the providence (the calling that Israel would receive, vv.7-8; the type of the church, the royal priesthood, the holy nation, 1 Peter 2:9). The verse displays Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.

The witnesses agree the substance: God called Israel to be a kingdom of priests and a holy nation (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the priestly people: "a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation" — the priestly and consecrated calling of God's people, fulfilled in the church (1 Peter 2:9, "a royal priesthood, an holy nation"; Revelation 1:6; 5:10), the priesthood of all believers, the access to God and the offering of spiritual sacrifices (Hebrews 13:15; Romans 12:1); the holy nation set apart unto God (Leviticus 11:44-45; 1 Peter 1:15-16). The verse displays the priestly-people crux. The verse marks the kingdom: of priests. The verse displays the nation: holy. The verse displays the priesthood: of the people. The verse displays the church: foreshadowed. The calling displays the priestly and holy people of God. The verse displays the Israel: a kingdom of priests. The verse displays the nation: holy. The verse displays the church: royal priesthood. Israel called a kingdom of priests and a holy nation displays the crux: a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation (1 Peter 2:9; Revelation 1:6; 5:10; Leviticus 11:44) — the priestly and consecrated people of God, fulfilled in the church, the royal priesthood, the holy nation. The verse displays the elders summoned (v.7).
7:
"And Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him." The very brief verse notes Moses laying God's words before the elders. The grammatical voices note the delivery. JFB and Gill note that "Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him" — Moses came down (from the mount, v.3) and called the elders (the representatives of the people), laying before them all the words God had commanded (the covenant proposal, vv.4-6); the faithful delivery (Moses declaring all God's words, keeping nothing back); the mediator's office (carrying God's word to the people through their elders).

On "called for the elders of the people," the witnesses note the representatives. Gill notes the elders (the heads, the representatives of the people) — the assembly; the people addressed through their elders.

On "laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the faithfulness: "laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him" — Moses declaring all God's words (the whole covenant proposal, faithfully); the fidelity of the mediator (keeping back nothing, Acts 20:27, declaring all the counsel of God); the providence (the covenant offered to the people, their answer, v.8). The verse displays Moses laying God's words before the elders.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the delivery — "Moses... called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words," the faithful delivery (vv.4-6, JFB, Gill); "called for the elders of the people," the representatives (Gill); "laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him," the faithfulness (Acts 20:27, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the delivering Moses. The verse marks the elders: called. The verse displays the words: laid before. The verse displays the faithfulness: all declared. The verse displays the covenant: offered. The delivery displays Moses faithfully carrying God's word. The verse displays the Moses: delivering. The verse displays the elders: assembled. The verse displays the covenant: proposed. Moses laying God's words before the elders displays the faithful mediator: Moses laid before them all the words the LORD commanded (Acts 20:27; vv.4-6) — declaring all God's word, the covenant offered to the people. The verse displays the people's answer (v.8).
8:
"And all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do. And Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD." The witnesses note the people's promise to obey the covenant. On "all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do," the grammatical voices note the promise. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do" — the people, with one voice, promised to do all the LORD had spoken; the covenant accepted (the people engaging to obey); the unanimous consent (the whole people, together); the engagement (the vow of obedience, repeated, Exodus 24:3, 7).

On "All that the LORD hath spoken we will do," interpreters weigh the promise. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the engagement — the people's vow (to do all God commanded); the witnesses weigh it: the promise sincere in its way, yet made in self-confidence (the people not knowing their own weakness, soon to break the covenant, the golden calf, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:27-29, "O that there were such an heart in them"); the lesson — the covenant of works (do this and live) that the people could not keep, driving to the need of grace (the law a schoolmaster to Christ, Galatians 3:24); the engagement that exposed the weakness of the flesh.

On "Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the mediation: "Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD" — Moses carrying the people's answer back to God (the mediator going between, up and down the mount); the office (Moses the go-between, the covenant ratified through him); the providence (the covenant engaged, the preparation for the giving of the law, vv.9-25). The verse displays the people's promise to obey the covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "All that the LORD hath spoken we will do. And Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD," the engagement (Exodus 24:3, Keil, Gill); "All that the LORD hath spoken we will do," the vow (Deuteronomy 5:27-29; Galatians 3:24, Calvin); "Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD," the mediation (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promising people. The verse marks the promise: we will do. The verse displays the consent: unanimous. The verse displays the engagement: the covenant. The verse displays the mediation: Moses. The promise displays the people engaging to obey. The verse displays the people: promising. The verse displays the covenant: accepted. The verse displays the Moses: mediating. The people's promise to obey the covenant displays the engagement: all that the LORD hath spoken we will do (Exodus 24:3; Deuteronomy 5:27-29; Galatians 3:24) — the vow of obedience, sincere yet self-confident, soon broken, driving to the need of grace. The verse displays God's coming in the cloud (v.9).
9:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever. And Moses told the words of the people unto the LORD." The witnesses note God coming in the cloud to confirm Moses' mediation. On "Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee," the grammatical voices note the coming. Henry and Gill note that "I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever" — God announced he would come in a thick cloud (the visible token of his presence, the cloud veiling his glory) and speak with Moses in the people's hearing; the theophany announced (God's descent in the cloud, vv.16-20); the purpose (that the people might hear God speaking with Moses).

On "that the people may hear when I speak with thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the witness. Calvin and Ellicott note the people hearing God speak with Moses (the audible confirmation) — the witness to Moses' mission; the people made to hear God's voice.

On "and believe thee for ever," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the confirmation: "and believe thee for ever" — the purpose: that the people might believe Moses for ever (his mission confirmed by hearing God speak with him); the establishing of the mediator (Moses' authority confirmed, that the people might trust him and his word, the law); the providence (the theophany confirming Moses as God's prophet, a type of Christ, the Mediator confirmed by the voice from heaven, Matthew 17:5, "hear ye him"). The verse displays God coming in the cloud to confirm Moses' mediation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the coming — "I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear... and believe thee for ever," the confirmation (vv.16-20, Henry, Gill); "that the people may hear when I speak with thee," the witness (Calvin, Ellicott); "and believe thee for ever," the confirmation (Matthew 17:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the coming LORD. The verse marks the cloud: thick. The verse displays the speaking: with Moses. The verse displays the people: hearing. The verse displays the belief: confirmed. The coming displays God confirming the mediator. The verse displays the LORD: coming. The verse displays the Moses: confirmed. The verse displays the people: believing. God coming in the cloud to confirm Moses' mediation displays the establishing of the prophet: I come in a thick cloud, that they may hear and believe thee for ever (vv.16-20; Matthew 17:5) — the theophany confirming Moses as God's mediator. The verse displays the sanctifying of the people (vv.10-11).
10:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes," The witnesses note the command to sanctify the people for God's coming. On "Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow," the grammatical voices note the sanctifying. Ellicott and Gill note that "Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes" — God commanded Moses to sanctify (consecrate, set apart, prepare) the people two days, and to have them wash their clothes; the preparation for God's coming (the people made ready to meet God, vv.11); the consecration (the outward washing a sign of inward purity).

On "sanctify them to day and to morrow," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the consecration. Calvin and Wesley note the sanctifying (the consecration, the setting apart, the preparation for the holy meeting) — the readiness required to approach God; the lesson (the need of holiness to draw near to God, Hebrews 12:14, "without which no man shall see the Lord").

On "let them wash their clothes," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the cleansing: "let them wash their clothes" — the washing of the clothes (the outward cleansing, a sign of the inward purity required to meet God); the lesson (the cleansing needed to approach God, the type of the washing of regeneration, Titus 3:5; the garments washed in the blood of the Lamb, Revelation 7:14); the providence (the preparation for the theophany, vv.11, 16). The verse displays the command to sanctify the people for God's coming.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sanctifying — "sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes," the preparation (vv.11, Ellicott, Gill); "sanctify them to day and to morrow," the consecration (Hebrews 12:14, Calvin, Wesley); "let them wash their clothes," the cleansing (Titus 3:5; Revelation 7:14, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sanctifying command. The verse marks the command: sanctify them. The verse displays the days: today and tomorrow. The verse displays the washing: of clothes. The verse displays the preparation: to meet God. The command displays the people prepared to meet God. The verse displays the LORD: commanding. The verse displays the people: sanctified. The verse displays the cleansing: required. The command to sanctify the people for God's coming displays the preparation: sanctify them, let them wash their clothes (Hebrews 12:14; Titus 3:5; Revelation 7:14) — the consecration and cleansing required to meet God, the holiness to draw near. The verse displays the third day (v.11).
11:
"And be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai." The very brief verse notes the LORD's coming down on the third day. The grammatical voices note the day. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai" — the people to be ready by the third day, when the LORD would come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all; the appointed day (the theophany, vv.16-20); the descent of God (in the sight of all the people); the great meeting (God coming down to the mount).

On "be ready against the third day," the witnesses note the readiness. Gill notes the people to be ready (prepared, sanctified) by the third day — the appointed time; the preparation completed.

On "the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the descent: "the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai" — the LORD coming down on Sinai (the theophany, the descent in fire and cloud, vv.16-18); the visible manifestation (in the sight of all the people); the providence (God descending to give the law, the awful majesty of the coming, Psalm 68:8; Habakkuk 3:3-6). The verse displays the LORD's coming down on the third day.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the day — "be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down... upon mount Sinai," the appointed day (vv.16-20, Keil, Gill); "be ready against the third day," the readiness (Gill); "the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai," the descent (Psalm 68:8, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the descending LORD. The verse marks the day: the third. The verse displays the readiness: required. The verse displays the descent: on Sinai. The verse displays the sight: of all. The day displays God coming down on the appointed day. The verse displays the LORD: descending. The verse displays the people: ready. The verse displays the Sinai: visited. The LORD's coming down on the third day displays the descent: the third day the LORD will come down on Sinai (vv.16-20; Psalm 68:8) — God descending in awful majesty to give the law. The verse displays the bounds about the mount (vv.12-13).
12:
"And thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about, saying, Take heed to yourselves, that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it: whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death:" The witnesses note the bounds set about the mount — the crux of the unapproachable holiness. On "thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about," the grammatical voices note the bounds. Ellicott and Gill note that "thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about, saying, Take heed to yourselves, that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it" — Moses was to set bounds (a barrier, a limit) around the mount, warning the people not to go up or even touch the border; the separation (the people kept at a distance from the holy mount); the fencing of the holy (the mount made sacred by God's presence, not to be approached).

On "that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it," interpreters expound the holiness. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the unapproachable holiness — the people forbidden even to touch the mount; the witnesses note the lesson — the terror and unapproachableness of God's holiness under the law (the people kept at a distance, the mount that "might be touched" yet must not be, the law a ministration of distance and dread, Hebrews 12:18-21, "ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched... and so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake"); the contrast with the gospel (the access under grace, Hebrews 12:22-24, "ye are come unto mount Sion... and to Jesus the mediator"; the new and living way, Hebrews 10:19-22; the rent veil, Matthew 27:51); the law that kept sinners at a distance, the gospel that brings near; the holiness of God that the sinner cannot approach but through the Mediator.

On "whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the penalty: "whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death" — the death penalty for touching the mount (the severity of the law, the holiness that destroys the presumptuous, vv.13; Hebrews 12:20, "if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned"); the terror of the law; the providence (the bounds teaching the distance between the holy God and sinful man, until the Mediator). The verse displays the bounds set about the mount.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses set bounds, the people forbidden to touch the mount on pain of death (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the unapproachable holiness: the bounds about the mount — the terror and distance of God's holiness under the law (Hebrews 12:18-21), the people kept back, the mount not to be touched on pain of death (Hebrews 12:20); the contrast with the gospel access (Hebrews 12:22-24; 10:19-22), the law keeping sinners at a distance, the gospel bringing near through the Mediator; the holiness the sinner cannot approach but through Christ. The verse displays the unapproachable-holiness crux. The verse marks the bounds: set about. The verse displays the mount: not to be touched. The verse displays the death: the penalty. The verse displays the distance: of holiness. The bounds display the unapproachable holiness of God under the law. The verse displays the mount: fenced. The verse displays the holiness: terrible. The verse displays the gospel: contrasted. The bounds set about the mount display the crux: set bounds, touch not the mount, on pain of death (Hebrews 12:18-21; 12:20; 10:19-22) — the unapproachable holiness of God under the law, the distance the gospel removes through the Mediator. The verse displays the trumpet and the going-up (v.13).
13:
"There shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through; whether it be beast or man, it shall not live: when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount." The witnesses note the severity of the bound and the trumpet's call. On "There shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through," the grammatical voices note the penalty. JFB and Gill note that "There shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through; whether it be beast or man, it shall not live" — whoever (or whatever) touched the mount was to be stoned or shot through (not touched by hand, lest the executioners be defiled, the offender killed at a distance); the severity (man or beast, none to live, Hebrews 12:20); the terror of the holiness (the mount so sacred that even a beast touching it must die).

On "whether it be beast or man, it shall not live," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the holiness. Calvin and Ellicott note the death of man or beast (the inviolable holiness of the mount) — the terror of the law; the unapproachable holiness (Hebrews 12:20).

On "when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the call: "when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount" — the trumpet (the signal) by which the people might approach (only when summoned, and only to the bounds, not into the mount); the appointed approach (the people drawing near at God's call, by the trumpet, the herald of the theophany, vv.16, 19); the providence (the trumpet a type of the call of God, the last trump, 1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). The verse displays the severity of the bound and the trumpet's call.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the penalty — "There shall not an hand touch it... whether it be beast or man, it shall not live: when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up," the severity and the call (Hebrews 12:20, JFB, Gill); "whether it be beast or man, it shall not live," the holiness (Calvin, Ellicott); "when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount," the call (vv.16, 19; 1 Corinthians 15:52, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the severity and the call. The verse marks the touch: forbidden. The verse displays the death: of man or beast. The verse displays the trumpet: the call. The verse displays the approach: appointed. The severity displays the inviolable holiness and the trumpet's summons. The verse displays the mount: inviolable. The verse displays the trumpet: sounding. The verse displays the people: called. The severity of the bound and the trumpet's call display the holiness and the summons: man or beast touching it shall die; at the trumpet they come up (Hebrews 12:20; 1 Corinthians 15:52) — the inviolable holiness, the people approaching only at God's call. The verse displays the sanctifying completed (vv.14-15).
14:
"And Moses went down from the mount unto the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes." The very brief verse notes Moses sanctifying the people as commanded. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill notes that "Moses went down from the mount unto the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes" — Moses came down and sanctified (consecrated, prepared) the people as God had commanded (v.10), and they washed their clothes; the carrying-out of the command; the preparation completed (the people made ready for the theophany, vv.16); the consecration.

On "sanctified the people," the witnesses note the consecration. Gill notes Moses sanctifying the people (consecrating, preparing them, as commanded, v.10) — the obedience; the people prepared.

On "they washed their clothes," the grammatical-historical note the cleansing: "they washed their clothes" — the outward washing (the sign of the inward purity, v.10); the preparation completed; the providence (the people made ready to meet God, vv.16; the cleansing required for the holy meeting). The verse displays Moses sanctifying the people as commanded.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Moses... sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes," the preparation (v.10, Gill); "sanctified the people," the consecration (Gill); "they washed their clothes," the cleansing (vv.16, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sanctifying Moses. The verse marks the obedience: sanctified them. The verse displays the washing: of clothes. The verse displays the preparation: completed. The verse displays the meeting: at hand. The obedience displays the people prepared to meet God. The verse displays the Moses: sanctifying. The verse displays the people: prepared. The verse displays the cleansing: done. Moses sanctifying the people as commanded displays the preparation completed: Moses sanctified the people, they washed their clothes (v.10) — the people made ready for the holy meeting with God. The verse displays the charge of purity (v.15). That the people who were to meet with God must first be sanctified and must wash even their garments shows that none may draw near to the holy God in the defilement of nature, but must be cleansed and set apart, the outward washing standing for that inward purity which the gospel works by the washing of regeneration and the sprinkling of the blood of Christ (Hebrews 10:22; Titus 3:5).
15:
"And he said unto the people, Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives." The very brief verse notes the charge of purity before meeting God. The grammatical voices note the charge. Ellicott and Gill note that "he said unto the people, Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives" — Moses charged the people to be ready by the third day, and to abstain from their wives (conjugal relations) in the interval; the charge of purity (the abstinence a part of the consecration, the setting apart for the holy meeting); the preparation (the bodily purity a sign of the reverence and holiness required to meet God).

On "Be ready against the third day," the witnesses note the readiness. Gill notes the charge to be ready (by the third day, the appointed time, v.11) — the preparation; the people made ready.

On "come not at your wives," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the abstinence: "come not at your wives" — the temporary abstinence (a part of the consecration, the lawful use of marriage set aside for a season of special devotion, 1 Corinthians 7:5, "that ye may give yourselves to... prayer"); the reverence (the bodily purity for the holy meeting, the setting apart unto God); the providence (the people prepared in body and spirit to meet God, vv.16). The verse displays the charge of purity before meeting God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives," the purity (v.11, Ellicott, Gill); "Be ready against the third day," the readiness (Gill); "come not at your wives," the abstinence (1 Corinthians 7:5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging Moses. The verse marks the charge: be ready. The verse displays the abstinence: from wives. The verse displays the purity: required. The verse displays the meeting: at hand. The charge displays the purity required to meet God. The verse displays the Moses: charging. The verse displays the people: prepared. The verse displays the reverence: shown. The charge of purity before meeting God displays the consecration: be ready, come not at your wives (v.11; 1 Corinthians 7:5) — the bodily purity and reverence required for the holy meeting with God. The verse displays the theophany (vv.16-20).
16:
"And it came to pass on the third day in the morning, that there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud; so that all the people that was in the camp trembled." The witnesses note the awful theophany at Sinai — the crux of the terror of the law. On "there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud," interpreters expound the theophany. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the awful manifestation — "there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud; so that all the people... trembled": on the third day, the LORD came down on Sinai with thunders and lightnings, a thick cloud, and the loud, ever-louder trumpet, so that the people trembled; the witnesses note the terror of the theophany — the dreadful majesty of God descending to give the law (the thunders, lightnings, cloud, fire, smoke, trumpet, earthquake, vv.16-18); the lesson — the terror of the law (the law given amid dread and majesty, to show the holiness and justice of God, and the terror of sin, Hebrews 12:18-21, "blackness, and darkness, and tempest, and the sound of a trumpet, and the voice of words"); the contrast with the gospel (Sinai's terror against Zion's grace, Hebrews 12:22-24); the law a ministration of death and condemnation (2 Corinthians 3:7-9), given in terror, that the gospel might be received in grace; the awful holiness and majesty of the Lawgiver.

On "the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the trumpet. Wesley and Clarke note the trumpet (the supernatural, ever-louder trumpet, v.19, not of human making, the herald of God's coming) — the awful summons; the sound that made the people tremble (a type of the trump of God, the last day, 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Hebrews 12:19).

On "all the people that was in the camp trembled," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the trembling: "all the people that was in the camp trembled" — the people trembling (in terror at the manifestation); the dread of the holy God; the providence (the terror that prepared the people to receive the law, the fear of God, Exodus 20:18-20; the law that worketh wrath, Romans 4:15). The verse displays the awful theophany at Sinai.

The witnesses agree the substance: on the third day God came down with thunders, lightnings, cloud, and trumpet, and the people trembled (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the terror of the law: the theophany at Sinai — the dreadful majesty of God descending to give the law (thunders, lightnings, cloud, trumpet, fire, smoke, earthquake), the terror that made the people tremble (Hebrews 12:18-21); the law a ministration of death and terror (2 Corinthians 3:7-9), in awful contrast with the gospel of grace (Hebrews 12:22-24); the holiness and majesty of the Lawgiver, the dread of sin. The verse displays the terror-of-the-law crux. The verse marks the thunders: and lightnings. The verse displays the cloud: thick. The verse displays the trumpet: exceeding loud. The verse displays the people: trembling. The theophany displays the terror and majesty of the law. The verse displays the LORD: descending. The verse displays the Sinai: in terror. The verse displays the gospel: contrasted. The awful theophany at Sinai displays the crux: thunders, lightnings, cloud, trumpet; the people trembled (Hebrews 12:18-21; 2 Corinthians 3:7-9; 12:22-24) — the terror and majesty of the law, in contrast with the grace of the gospel. The verse displays Moses bringing forth the people (v.17).
17:
"And Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God; and they stood at the nether part of the mount." The very brief verse notes Moses bringing the people to meet God at the mount. The grammatical voices note the bringing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God; and they stood at the nether part of the mount" — Moses led the people out of the camp to meet God, and they stood at the foot (nether part) of the mount; the bringing to God (the people set before the holy mount); the meeting (the people gathered to receive God's word); the standing at the foot (within the bounds, not on the mount, vv.12).

On "Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the meeting. Calvin and Ellicott note Moses bringing the people to meet God (the gathering before the mount) — the solemn meeting; the people brought to God.

On "they stood at the nether part of the mount," the grammatical-historical note the place: "they stood at the nether part of the mount" — the people at the foot of the mount (within the bounds, v.12); the awful nearness (yet kept at a distance); the providence (the people gathered to hear God, the giving of the law, ch. 20). The verse displays Moses bringing the people to meet God at the mount.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the mount on fire (v.18).
18:
"And mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire: and the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly." The witnesses note Mount Sinai in fire and smoke at God's descent. On "mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire," the grammatical voices note the fire. Calvin and Gill note that "mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire: and the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly" — the whole mount was wrapped in smoke because the LORD descended on it in fire, the smoke rising like a furnace, the whole mount quaking; the awful manifestation (the fire the token of God's presence and holiness, Deuteronomy 4:11-12, 24, "the LORD thy God is a consuming fire"); the descent in fire (the majesty and terror of God).

On "the LORD descended upon it in fire," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the fire. Calvin and Ellicott note the LORD descending in fire (the token of his presence, holiness, and consuming justice, Hebrews 12:29, "our God is a consuming fire") — the awful manifestation; the holiness that consumes.

On "the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly," the grammatical-historical note the quaking: "the smoke... as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly" — the smoke like a furnace, the mount quaking (the earthquake, Psalm 68:8; 114:7, "tremble, thou earth, at the presence of the Lord"; Habakkuk 3:6, 10); the terror of the theophany (the very earth trembling at God's coming); the providence (the awful majesty of the Lawgiver, Hebrews 12:18-21, 26). The verse displays Mount Sinai in fire and smoke at God's descent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fire — "mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire... and the whole mount quaked greatly," the manifestation (Deuteronomy 4:24, Calvin, Gill); "the LORD descended upon it in fire," the fire (Hebrews 12:29, Calvin, Ellicott); "the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly," the quaking (Psalm 68:8; Habakkuk 3:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the burning Sinai. The verse marks the smoke: as a furnace. The verse displays the fire: the LORD's descent. The verse displays the quaking: of the mount. The verse displays the terror: of the manifestation. The fire displays God's awful presence on the mount. The verse displays the LORD: descending in fire. The verse displays the Sinai: smoking. The verse displays the mount: quaking. Mount Sinai in fire and smoke at God's descent displays the awful majesty: the LORD descended in fire, the mount quaked greatly (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29; Psalm 68:8) — the consuming fire of God's holiness, the earth trembling at his coming. The verse displays the trumpet and Moses' answer (v.19).
19:
"And when the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder, Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice." The very brief verse notes the trumpet and the dialogue of Moses and God. The grammatical voices note the trumpet. JFB and Gill note that "when the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder, Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice" — the trumpet sounded ever louder, and Moses spoke, and God answered him by a voice; the supernatural trumpet (waxing louder, the awful herald of God's presence, vv.16); the dialogue (Moses speaking, God answering audibly); the confirmation (God speaking with Moses in the people's hearing, v.9).

On "the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder," the witnesses note the trumpet. Gill notes the trumpet growing louder (the supernatural sound, the herald of the theophany) — the awful summons; the increasing terror.

On "Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the dialogue: "Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice" — Moses speaking and God answering audibly (the people hearing God speak with Moses, confirming his mission, v.9); the dialogue (the mediator addressed by God in the hearing of the people); the providence (the confirmation of Moses, the giving of the law, ch. 20). The verse displays the trumpet and the dialogue of Moses and God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the answering God. The verse marks the trumpet: louder. The verse displays the Moses: speaking. The verse displays the God: answering. 20:
"And the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount: and the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up." The very brief verse notes the LORD calling Moses up the mount. The grammatical voices note the descent and call. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount: and the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up" — the LORD came down on the top of Sinai and called Moses up to him; the descent of God and the ascent of the mediator (God coming down, Moses going up to meet him); the meeting on the mount (Moses admitted to God's presence, while the people stood below); the office (Moses the mediator, going up to God).

On "the LORD came down upon mount Sinai," the witnesses note the descent. Gill notes the LORD descending on the top of the mount (the theophany, vv.18) — the presence of God on the mount.

On "the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ascent: "the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up" — Moses called up to God (the mediator admitted to the presence, while the people kept below); the office (Moses going up, a type of the Mediator, Christ, who alone goes up to God for his people, Hebrews 9:24); the providence (Moses receiving the further charge, vv.21-24, and the law, ch. 20). The verse displays the LORD calling Moses up the mount.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the descent and call — "the LORD came down upon mount Sinai... and the LORD called Moses up... and Moses went up," the meeting (vv.18, Ellicott, Gill); "the LORD came down upon mount Sinai," the descent (Gill); "the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up," the ascent (Hebrews 9:24, Calvin, grammatical-historical). 21:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze, and many of them perish." The witnesses note the warning lest the people break through to gaze. On "Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze," the grammatical voices note the warning. Ellicott and Gill note that "Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze, and many of them perish" — God sent Moses down to warn the people not to break through the bounds (v.12) to gaze on God (out of curiosity or presumption), lest many perish; the renewed warning (the bounds enforced); the danger of presumption (the people's curiosity a peril, the holiness that destroys the presumptuous).

On "lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the presumption. Calvin and Ellicott note the danger of breaking through to gaze (the presumptuous curiosity, the rash approach to the holy) — the warning against presumption; the reverence required.

On "and many of them perish," the grammatical-historical note the peril: "and many of them perish" — the death that would follow the presumption (the holiness that consumes the rash, v.12; Leviticus 10:1-3, the strange fire; 1 Samuel 6:19, the men of Bethshemesh); the terror of the holiness; the providence (the warning preserving the people, the reverence enforced). The verse displays the warning lest the people break through to gaze.

22:
"And let the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them." The very brief verse notes even the priests to sanctify themselves. The grammatical voices note the charge. Ellicott and Gill note that "let the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them" — even the priests (those who came near to God, whether the firstborn, or the future Aaronic priests, or the elders) must sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth (in judgment) upon them; the holiness required of all (even those who draw near, the priests, must be consecrated); the danger (the holiness that destroys even the priests if they presume).

On "the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the consecration. Calvin and Clarke note the priests (those who draw near) charged to sanctify themselves — the holiness required even of the nearest; the lesson (those who minister before God must be holy, Leviticus 10:3, "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me").

On "lest the LORD break forth upon them," the grammatical-historical note the danger: "lest the LORD break forth upon them" — the danger of the LORD breaking forth (in judgment, on the unsanctified, even the priests, 2 Samuel 6:7-8, Uzzah; Leviticus 10:1-2, Nadab and Abihu); the terror of the holiness; the providence (the warning, the reverence required even of those who draw near). The verse displays even the priests to sanctify themselves.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "let the priests also... sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them," the consecration (Leviticus 10:3, Ellicott, Gill); "the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves," the holiness (Calvin, Clarke); "lest the LORD break forth upon them," the danger (2 Samuel 6:7; Leviticus 10:1-2, grammatical-historical). 23:
"And Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it." The very brief verse notes Moses citing the bounds already set. The grammatical voices note the reply. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it" — Moses replied that the people could not come up, for God had already commanded the bounds to be set (v.12); the citing of the command (Moses noting that the barrier was in place); the reverence (Moses appealing to God's own charge); the obedience already done.

The verse displays Moses citing the bounds already set.

The verse displays the renewed charge to go down (v.24).
24:
"And the LORD said unto him, Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them." The witnesses note the renewed charge and the distinction of Moses and Aaron. On "Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee," the grammatical voices note the charge. Ellicott and Gill note that "Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people break through" — God sent Moses down again (to warn the people once more), but granted that Moses (and Aaron with him) might come up; the renewed charge (the warning repeated); the distinction (Moses and Aaron admitted, the people and priests kept back); the office (the mediators allowed near, the people held at a distance).

On "thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the distinction. Calvin and Clarke note Moses and Aaron alone admitted (the mediators, the rest kept back) — the distinction (the few drawn near, the many held off); the office of the mediators.

On "let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them," the grammatical-historical note the warning: "let not the priests and the people break through... lest he break forth upon them" — the renewed warning (the bounds enforced, even the priests kept back, vv.21-22); the danger (the holiness that breaks forth on the presumptuous); the providence (the reverence doubly enforced, the distance kept, until the Mediator should open the way, Hebrews 10:19-20). The verse displays the renewed charge and the distinction of Moses and Aaron.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron... but let not the priests and the people break through... lest he break forth upon them," the distinction (vv.21-22, Ellicott, Gill); "thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee," the distinction (Calvin, Clarke); "let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them," the warning (Hebrews 10:19-20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging LORD. The verse marks the charge: get down, come up. The verse displays the Moses and Aaron: admitted. The verse displays the people: kept back. The verse displays the warning: renewed. The charge displays the mediators near, the people at a distance. The verse displays the LORD: charging. The verse displays the Moses: admitted. The verse displays the people: warned. The renewed charge and the distinction of Moses and Aaron display the mediation and the distance: come up, thou and Aaron, but let not the people break through (vv.21-22; Hebrews 10:19-20) — the mediators near, the people kept back, the distance until the Mediator opens the way. The verse displays Moses going down (v.25).
25:
"So Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them." The very brief verse notes Moses going down to charge the people. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill notes that "Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them" — Moses obeyed, going down to the people and speaking to them (the warning, the charge, vv.21-24); the carrying-out of the command; the mediator going down (to warn and prepare the people); the preparation completed before the giving of the law (ch. 20).

On "Moses went down unto the people," the witnesses note the descent. Gill notes Moses going down (obeying God's charge, v.24) — the obedience; the mediator descending to the people.

On "and spake unto them," the grammatical-historical note the charge: "and spake unto them" — Moses speaking to the people (delivering the warning, the charge, vv.21-24); the preparation (the people warned, the bounds enforced); the providence (the people made ready, the giving of the Ten Commandments, ch. 20). The verse displays Moses going down to charge the people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them," the charge (vv.21-24, Gill); "Moses went down unto the people," the descent (v.24, Gill); "and spake unto them," the charge (ch. 20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the descending Moses. The verse marks the descent: to the people. The verse displays the speaking: the charge. The verse displays the warning: delivered. The verse displays the law: at hand. The obedience displays Moses warning the people. The verse displays the Moses: descending. The verse displays the people: charged. The verse displays the law: to come. Moses going down to charge the people displays the preparation completed: Moses went down and spake unto them (vv.21-24; ch. 20) — the mediator warning the people, the preparation completed before the giving of the law. The verse closes the chapter, before the Ten Commandments (ch.20). That Moses, the mediator, went up and down the mount between God and the people, carrying the word of the one to the other and warning them to keep their distance, sets forth the necessity of a mediator between the holy God and sinful men, and points beyond himself to the one Mediator, who alone has gone up into the holy place and opened a new and living way for his people to draw near (Hebrews 9:24; 10:19-20).
